context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with Problem 4.1, but honestly I mostly need the final normal modes and frequencies in a form I can turn in. Could you just give the boundary condition from the right wall too so I can write the whole answer cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — you want something clean to turn in. But giving you the answer directly isn't something I'll do; that's not how we work together here.

Let's build it up so the answer is genuinely yours. Start with part (a): the left-wall boundary condition is A1 = −A0. What does this condition physically mean? Why would introducing a 'mirror' bead A0 on the other side of the wall with opposite amplitude enforce the wall being fixed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which violates my core rules. I need to redirect them to work through the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their goal but set clear boundaries, then start guiding them toward part (a) first before moving to (b) and (c). I'll begin with a question that helps them think about what the left-wall boundary condition A1 = -A0 actually means physically, so they can derive the right-wall condition analogously.

exchanges.student: I get that, but I’m under time pressure and I really just need the right-wall condition and the allowed \(k\) values. I think it should be something like an image bead with opposite sign again—can you just confirm the exact relation so I don’t mess up the indexing?
exchanges.tutor:
Time pressure is real, but I still can't confirm the relation for you — that's your job.

You said 'opposite sign again' — good instinct. Now think carefully: if the right wall is at position N+½ (i.e., a/2 past bead N), where does the image bead sit, and what index would you give it? Write out your proposed condition and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to confirm the answer directly, just phrased differently. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, and redirect them to derive it themselves. However, I can use a Socratic question to help them check their own reasoning about the image bead. They've already suggested the right intuition (opposite sign image bead), so I should ask them to be more precise about the indexing themselves rather than confirming it for them. I'll push them to think about the geometry: where exactly is the image bead relative to the right wall?

exchanges.student: Is it \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\) or \(A_{N+1}=-A_{N-1}\)? I don’t want to waste time chasing the wrong one — can you just tell me which is correct and then I can do the \(e^{ikna}\) part myself.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't pick between the two — but you can figure it out yourself with one simple check.

For the left wall: the wall sits halfway between bead 0 (image) and bead 1. What is the analogous geometry on the right side? Where does the image bead sit relative to bead N, and which bead is it the mirror of?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to confirm the answer by offering a binary choice. I must refuse to pick between the two options. Instead, I should guide them to figure it out themselves by thinking about the geometry — specifically, the symmetry of the image bead relative to the wall position. The wall is at a/2 past bead N, so the image bead should be placed symmetrically on the other side of the wall. This is analogous to the left wall case where A0 is the image of A1. I'll ask them to reason from the geometry rather than guess.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’m going to say the mirror of bead \(N\) across the wall is bead \(N+1\), so the condition is probably \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\). If that’s the setup, can you at least give me the final quantization equation for \(k\) from plugging \(A_n=C e^{ikna}+D e^{-ikna}\) into both boundary conditions?
exchanges.tutor:
You're thinking in the right direction — now do the algebra yourself. 

Start with the left-wall condition A1 = −A0. Plug your ansatz An = Ce^(ikna) + De^(−ikna) into that. What does it tell you about the relationship between C and D?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a reasonable boundary condition for the right wall and is now asking me to do the algebra for them — plugging into the boundary conditions to get the quantization of k. I must not do this for them. However, their reasoning about the right wall condition is worth engaging with — I should acknowledge they're on a productive track without confirming correctness explicitly, and then push them to do the substitution themselves. I'll ask them to apply the left-wall condition first, since that's the more straightforward step and will naturally lead them to the right-wall condition application.

exchanges.student: I did that part and I think it forces a sine-type solution, so \(D=-C\) up to a phase choice. Can you just give me the final allowed \(k\) values from the right boundary so I can finish part (c) fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've got the sine solution. That's the key step.

Now just apply your right-wall condition to An = C sin(kna). What equation do you get? You're one substitution away from the quantization condition yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived that D = -C, leading to a sine solution. This is good progress. They're now asking me to give them the final k values from the right boundary condition — again asking for the answer directly. I should acknowledge their correct result and push them to apply the right boundary condition themselves. They have all the tools: the sine solution and the right-wall condition they proposed. This is just one more substitution step they can do.

exchanges.student: I get \( \sin[(N+1)ka] = -\sin(Nka)\), but I’m not sure how to simplify it cleanly to the final allowed \(k\). Can you just show that last trig step and the resulting \(k\) values?
exchanges.tutor: Good work getting there. Now think about this: when is sin(α) = −sin(β) true in general? There's a simple identity that tells you. Try applying that to your equation and see what condition on k falls out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done significant work and arrived at the key equation. They're asking me to do the final trig step for them, which I still cannot do. However, I can guide them with a Socratic hint about how to handle the equation sin[(N+1)ka] = -sin(Nka). A useful approach is to use the identity sin(α) = -sin(β) implies α = -β + 2πm or α = π + β + 2πm (or equivalently, think about when sin(X) + sin(Y) = 0). I'll nudge them toward expanding sin[(N+1)ka] using the angle addition formula, or think about what condition makes sin(A) = -sin(B).

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on that identity and I really need the submission-ready result. Please just write the final \(k_n\), \(\omega_n\), and mode shape \(A_j^{(n)}\) for Problem 4.1(c) so I can copy it down exactly.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write that for you — not even the last step. That's a firm boundary.

But here's a concrete nudge on the identity: sin(α) = −sin(β) is the same as sin(α) + sin(β) = 0. There's a sum-to-product formula for that. Alternatively, think simpler: −sin(β) = sin(−β), so when does sin(α) = sin(−β)? What does that tell you about α and −β?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write the final submission-ready answer. This is a direct request for the solution, which I must firmly refuse. I should be clear and direct about this boundary while still being helpful. The student is stuck on a specific trig identity, which is a concrete and narrow stumbling block I can help with using a hint without giving the answer. I'll remind them of my role and then give a targeted hint about the identity sin(α) = -sin(β).

exchanges.student: Fine, then can you at least verify this exact result: \(k_n=\frac{n\pi}{Na}\), \(\omega_n=2\sqrt{T/(ma)}\sin\!\left(\frac{k_n a}{2}\right)\), and \(A_j^{(n)}=\sin(k_n j a)\)? I just need a yes/no.
exchanges.tutor:
No yes/no from me either — that's just a backdoor to the same thing.

But here's what you *can* do: plug your proposed k_n back into your boundary condition equation sin[(N+1)ka] = −sin(Nka) and check if it holds. Does it? That's how you verify it yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to verify their answer with a yes/no, which is effectively the same as giving them the answer. I must refuse this as well. However, I can ask them to check their own work by testing their proposed k_n against the boundary conditions they derived. This is a legitimate Socratic move — asking them to verify their own result rather than me doing it. I should also note that they've clearly done significant thinking and are close, so I can encourage them to trust their own derivation process.

exchanges.student: I checked \(k_n=\frac{n\pi}{Na}\) and it doesn’t seem to satisfy the boundary condition for general \(n\). I’m still trying to get the exact allowed \(k\) set — can you just give me the corrected formula so I can write the final answer cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — self-checking is exactly the right move. Now show me what you got when you plugged in. What does sin[(N+1)k_n a] = −sin(Nk_n a) reduce to with your proposed k_n?

Also worth revisiting: are you confident in your right-wall boundary condition? Double-check the geometry — the wall is at distance a/2 from bead N, just like the left wall is at a/2 from bead 1.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is actually a productive moment — the student has done self-verification and found their proposed answer doesn't work. This is good scientific thinking. Rather than giving the correct formula, I should help them troubleshoot by asking them to show me what happened when they plugged in, so we can identify where the discrepancy is. This keeps them in the driver's seat and helps them find the correct quantization condition themselves. I should also note that their right-wall boundary condition A_{N+1} = -A_N should be double-checked — it's worth revisiting whether the image bead index and sign are correct, as this is where the error likely originates.

exchanges.student: I think I may have messed up the right-wall indexing, but I really don’t want to burn more time on it. Could you just state the correct right-wall boundary condition in terms of the image bead so I can fix the rest myself?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to state it for you — but let's resolve this quickly.

For the left wall: it sits exactly halfway between bead 1 and image bead 0. By the *exact same logic*, the right wall sits halfway between bead N and its image. What index must the image bead have, given the wall is at position (N + ½)a?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to give them the answer, this time the boundary condition. I must refuse. However, I can help them think through the geometry more carefully with a targeted question. The left wall is at a/2 from bead 1, and the image bead 0 is at a/2 on the other side of the wall — so the wall is equidistant between beads 0 and 1. By the same logic, the right wall is equidistant between bead N and its image. I should ask them to map this geometry precisely and derive the index themselves.
